title: The Melian Dialogue: The Strong Do What They Can
description: Athens demands the surrender of Melos: the most brutal statement of political realism ever written.
introduction: In 416 BCE, Athens sent a fleet to the neutral island of Melos and demanded unconditional surrender. The ensuing dialogue, as reconstructed by Thucydides, strips away all pretense of justice in international relations. The Athenians state openly that might makes right, that the gods themselves follow this principle, and that the Melians' hope in justice is suicidal. When the Melians refuse to submit, Athens kills all the men and enslaves the women and children. The passage remains the foundational text of political realism.

translation.original_text: ἐπειδὴ... δίκαια μὲν ἐν τῷ ἀνθρωπείῳ λόγῳ ἀπὸ τῆς ἴσης ἀνάγκης κρίνεται, δυνατὰ δὲ οἱ προύχοντες πράσσουσι καὶ οἱ ἀσθενεῖς ξυγχωροῦσιν.
translation.text: Since... in the world of men, justice is only decided between equals in power, while the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must.

translation.original_text: ἡγούμεθα γὰρ τό τε θεῖον δόξῃ τὸ ἀνθρώπειόν τε σαφῶς διὰ παντὸς ὑπὸ φύσεως ἀναγκαίας, οὗ ἂν κρατῇ, ἄρχειν.
translation.text: We believe that the divine, by common opinion, and the human, by clear evidence, are both compelled by nature to rule wherever they can.
